narrative: As a Hispanic male applying to law school, you've shared your undergraduate GPA of 3.20, LSAT score of 19.30, lower-middle family income background, and plans to attend full-time, along with your school decile rankings from Year 1 and Year 3, both at the lowest 10th percentile. The admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on patterns from thousands of past students, estimated an 80% chance you'll fail the bar exam— a threshold that led to your application's rejection to protect the school's outcomes. This prediction wasn't about your potential or character, but a data-driven assessment weighing how similar profiles have fared historically. The three biggest factors pushing the model toward this "fail" prediction, in order of strongest influence, were your Year 3 school decile ranking, your LSAT score, and your Year 1 school decile ranking— all of which signaled higher risk compared to typical admitted students. Your Year 3 decile in the bottom 10% (far below the average 5th or 6th decile) had the most weight, as it strongly indicated challenges sustaining strong academic performance over time, a key bar exam predictor. Next, your LSAT score of 19.30— much lower than the typical 37— reinforced concerns about foundational skills needed for legal reasoning under pressure. Your Year 1 decile, also in the bottom 10% (versus an average around the 6th), added to this by highlighting early academic hurdles that often persist. While your undergrad GPA sat right at the average of 3.21 and full-time status aligns with most students, these weren't enough to offset the top concerns. Compared to students who typically pass the bar, your profile shows lower early and later school performance and test scores, patterns the model links to higher failure rates. This doesn't define you— many overcome similar starts through targeted effort. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, seeking academic coaching to improve decile rankings in advanced coursework, or exploring bridge programs that build bar prep skills before reapplying. You're taking a vital step by understanding this feedback, and with focus, you can reshape your path to success.
